title: Bandwidth by Category
spl:
index=security tag=web
| eval bytes=bytes_in+bytes_out
| lookup proxy_category url OUTPUT category
| stats sum(bytes) as b by category
| sort -b
description: When `app`/`url` category exists in CIM or via lookup — top GB by category.
value: When `app`/`url` category exists in CIM or via lookup — top GB by category.
implementation: Normalize CDN cache hits; exclude software update domains.
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` sum(Web.bytes_in) as bi, sum(Web.bytes_out) as bo from datamodel=Web.Web by Web.url
| eval bytes=bi+bo
knownFalsePositives: Web metrics skew from CDN cutovers, API load tests, and mobile app releases; compare user-agent and path baselines to avoid false app-security hunts.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Proxy → Web / Network_Traffic.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `bytes_in`, `bytes_out` on Web events.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Normalize CDN cache hits; exclude software update domains.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=security tag=web
| eval bytes=bytes_in+bytes_out
| lookup proxy_category url OUTPUT category
| stats sum(bytes) as b by category
| sort -b
```

Understanding this SPL

**Bandwidth by Category** — When `app`/`url` category exists in CIM or via lookup — top GB by category.

Documented **Data sources**: `bytes_in`, `bytes_out` on Web events. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Proxy → Web / Network_Traffic. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: security.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=security, tags. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **bytes** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by category** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` sum(Web.bytes_in) as bi, sum(Web.bytes_out) as bo from datamodel=Web.Web by Web.url
| eval bytes=bi+bo
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Bandwidth by Category** — When `app`/`url` category exists in CIM or via lookup — top GB by category.

Documented **Data sources**: `bytes_in`, `bytes_out` on Web events. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Proxy → Web / Network_Traffic. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Web.Web` — enable acceleration for that model.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **bytes** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Accelerate the CIM data model used in the optional tstats path, then run both the primary and `cimSpl` searches over a week that includes a known good baseline. Spot-check a few rows and tune thresholds; confirm no permission or tagging gap is hiding part of the population.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart (GB by category), Treemap.
grandmaExplanation: We use the same kind of check described for "Bandwidth by Category" once our data is shaped in a common, repeatable way across products. We help the team see trouble early, compare different tools fairly, and avoid building a one-off report for every vendor.
dataModelAcceleration: Enable Data Model Acceleration for the CIM `Web.Web` dataset after normalizing this feed to the model; otherwise the `cimSpl` tstats search may return no rows.
